questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Lights Out for Migration
Every spring and fall, thousands of birds migrate over the city of Fairmont at night. They use stars, moonlight, landforms, and instinct to guide their travel. Bright building lights can confuse them, especially during cloudy weather when natural sky cues are harder to see. Birds may circle lit buildings until they are exhausted or collide with windows they cannot recognize as barriers.
After volunteers found dozens of dead or injured birds downtown during one migration season, the city created a voluntary “Lights Out” program. Building owners were asked to turn off unnecessary lights above the fifth floor from midnight to dawn during peak migration weeks. The city also encouraged residents and businesses to close blinds, reduce decorative lighting, and place decals on large windows where collisions had occurred.
The program did not require every light to go dark. Emergency lighting, security lighting, and lights needed for overnight workers could remain on. The goal was to reduce avoidable light, not to make buildings unsafe or unusable.
The first year’s results were modest but promising. Volunteers reported fewer bird collisions in the blocks where most buildings participated. The city also learned that outreach mattered. Some building managers had not known that upper-floor lights could affect migrating birds. Once they understood the timing and purpose of the program, several agreed to participate the following season.
Fairmont’s experience shows that small changes in human habits can make cities safer for wildlife without stopping city life.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Fairmont’s voluntary “Lights Out” program reduced avoidable nighttime lighting to help migrating birds.
choices.B: Fairmont required every building to turn off emergency lighting all night.
choices.C: Migrating birds travel only during the daytime and are not affected by city lights.
choices.D: Building owners refused to participate after learning about bird migration.

questions.correctAnswerText: Fairmont’s voluntary “Lights Out” program reduced avoidable nighttime lighting to help migrating birds.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how Fairmont encouraged reduced lighting during migration to lower bird collisions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lights Out for Migration.”
Why are bright building lights especially confusing to migrating birds during cloudy weather?

choices.A: Natural sky cues are harder to see.
choices.B: Birds stop flying during every cloudy night.
choices.C: Building lights become colder during cloudy weather.
choices.D: Volunteers cannot count birds when clouds are present.

questions.correctAnswerText: Natural sky cues are harder to see.
questions.explanation: The passage states that cloudy weather makes natural sky cues harder for birds to see.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Lights Out for Migration.”
Which TWO actions were encouraged by the city?

choices.A: Turning off unnecessary lights above the fifth floor during peak migration weeks
choices.B: Closing blinds during migration periods
choices.C: Removing all windows from downtown buildings
choices.D: Keeping decorative lighting on all night
choices.E: Canceling all overnight work in office buildings

questions.correctAnswerText: Turning off unnecessary lights above the fifth floor during peak migration weeks | Closing blinds during migration periods
questions.explanation: Both actions were encouraged as part of the program.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lights Out for Migration.”
What can be inferred from the statement that outreach mattered?

choices.A: Some people were more willing to help after they understood the problem and the purpose of the program.
choices.B: Building managers already knew everything about bird migration before the program began.
choices.C: Volunteers stopped sharing information after the first year.
choices.D: The city discovered that education had no effect on participation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some people were more willing to help after they understood the problem and the purpose of the program.
questions.explanation: The passage says several building managers agreed to participate after learning why the program mattered.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lights Out for Migration.”
In the sentence “The first year’s results were modest but promising,” modest most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=limited but noticeable
questions.explanation: “Modest but promising” means the results were not dramatic but showed encouraging signs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lights Out for Migration.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Volunteers found dead or injured birds downtown during migration season.
tiles.B: Emergency and security lighting could remain on.
tiles.C: Fewer collisions were reported where most buildings participated.
tiles.D: The city banned all nighttime work in downtown buildings.

targets.T1: Evidence of the problem that led to the program
targets.T2: Evidence that the program considered safety needs
targets.T3: Evidence that participation may have helped

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Volunteers found dead or injured birds downtown during migration season.; T2=Emergency and security lighting could remain on.; T3=Fewer collisions were reported where most buildings participated.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches the problem, safety consideration, and possible effect of participation.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Hotel Lost-and-Found Procedure
To: Front Desk, Housekeeping, and Security Staff
From: Guest Services Manager
Subject: Updated Lost-and-Found Procedure
Beginning December 1, all found guest items must be logged before the end of the shift. Items may include clothing, chargers, jewelry, medication, luggage, documents, or personal electronics.
When an item is found, the employee should place it in a clear bag if possible and attach a label with the date, room or location, employee name, and brief item description. Valuable items, medication, passports, and identification cards must be taken directly to the security office. Do not leave these items in an unlocked housekeeping cart or at the front desk.
The front desk may contact a guest only after the item has been logged. Staff should describe the item generally and ask the guest to confirm details before arranging pickup or shipping. For example, say “a bracelet was found,” not “a silver bracelet with blue stones was found.” This helps confirm that the item belongs to the guest.
Items not claimed within 90 days will be handled according to company policy. Questions about shipping, storage, or valuable items should be directed to the guest services manager.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should label, secure, and record found guest items
choices.B: To tell staff to leave valuable items on housekeeping carts
choices.C: To require guests to describe every item before it is found
choices.D: To announce that the hotel will stop returning lost items

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should label, secure, and record found guest items
questions.explanation: The document provides instructions for handling lost-and-found items.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Hotel Lost-and-Found Procedure.”
Which item must be taken directly to the security office?

choices.A: A passport
choices.B: A paperback novel
choices.C: A pair of socks
choices.D: A hotel brochure

questions.correctAnswerText: A passport
questions.explanation: The procedure says passports must be taken directly to the security office.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Hotel Lost-and-Found Procedure.”
Which TWO details should be included on the label attached to a found item?

choices.A: The date
choices.B: The room or location where the item was found
choices.C: The guest’s credit card number
choices.D: The hotel’s weekly occupancy rate

questions.correctAnswerText: The date | The room or location where the item was found
questions.explanation: The label should include the date, room or location, employee name, and item description.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Hotel Lost-and-Found Procedure.”
Why should staff describe a found item generally when contacting a guest?

choices.A: To allow the guest to confirm details and help prove ownership
choices.B: To make the item harder for the guest to identify
choices.C: To avoid logging the item before calling the guest
choices.D: To encourage guests to claim items that are not theirs

questions.correctAnswerText: To allow the guest to confirm details and help prove ownership
questions.explanation: A general description helps confirm that the guest truly owns the item.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Hotel Lost-and-Found Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for giving only a general description of a found item?

choices.A: “Beginning December 1, all found guest items must be logged before the end of the shift.”
choices.B: “Valuable items, medication, passports, and identification cards must be taken directly to the security office.”
choices.C: “This helps confirm that the item belongs to the guest.”
choices.D: “Items not claimed within 90 days will be handled according to company policy.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “This helps confirm that the item belongs to the guest.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the reason for describing the item generally.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Offer Free Notary Hours
The county should offer free monthly notary hours at community centers. Many ordinary tasks require a notarized signature, including some school forms, housing documents, vehicle paperwork, medical authorization forms, and financial documents. People who do not know where to find a notary may delay important paperwork or pay fees they can barely afford.
Free notary hours would not replace legal advice. A notary verifies identity and witnesses signatures; a notary does not explain whether a document is legally wise to sign. The county could make this clear by posting signs and training staff to refer legal questions to appropriate services. The program would simply make a required administrative step easier.
The need is especially real for residents with limited transportation or inflexible work schedules. A person who works during bank hours may struggle to find a notary without missing wages. A parent completing a school form may need the service quickly. Community centers already serve as trusted locations for public programs, so they are practical places to host monthly notary sessions.
Some critics may argue that notary service is a small issue compared with housing, health care, or employment. But small barriers can still block important progress. A free notary program would be inexpensive, limited, and useful. It would help residents complete necessary paperwork without turning a simple signature into a larger problem.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: The county should offer free notary hours because notarized signatures are sometimes necessary and can create barriers for residents.
choices.B: The county should require every document to be notarized.
choices.C: Notaries should give legal advice about every form residents bring.
choices.D: Community centers should stop hosting public programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: The county should offer free notary hours because notarized signatures are sometimes necessary and can create barriers for residents.
questions.explanation: The author argues that free notary hours would help residents complete required paperwork.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Free Notary Hours.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that free notary hours would help with ordinary tasks?

choices.A: Many school, housing, vehicle, medical, and financial documents may require notarized signatures.
choices.B: A notary does not explain whether a document is legally wise to sign.
choices.C: Critics may argue that notary service is a small issue.
choices.D: Community centers host many public programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many school, housing, vehicle, medical, and financial documents may require notarized signatures.
questions.explanation: This evidence shows that notarization is connected to ordinary important tasks.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Offer Free Notary Hours.”
Which TWO limits or safeguards does the author suggest?

choices.A: Make clear that notary service does not replace legal advice.
choices.B: Train staff to refer legal questions to appropriate services.
choices.C: Allow notaries to approve documents without checking identity.
choices.D: Require residents to sign documents they do not understand.
choices.E: Charge extra fees for residents with limited transportation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Make clear that notary service does not replace legal advice. | Train staff to refer legal questions to appropriate services.
questions.explanation: These safeguards prevent the program from being confused with legal advice.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Free Notary Hours.”
How does the author respond to the idea that notary service is a small issue?

choices.A: By arguing that small barriers can still block important progress
choices.B: By claiming housing and health care are unimportant
choices.C: By saying notaries should replace attorneys
choices.D: By recommending that residents avoid paperwork

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that small barriers can still block important progress
questions.explanation: The author responds by explaining that even small administrative barriers can create serious delays.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Free Notary Hours.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 3: [D1]

D1: A survey at three community centers found that many residents had delayed forms because they could not find an affordable notary nearby.
The community center lobby has two bulletin boards and a drinking fountain.
Some residents prefer to use blue ink when signing forms.

questions.correct: D1=A survey at three community centers found that many residents had delayed forms because they could not find an affordable notary nearby.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A survey at three community centers found that many residents had delayed forms because they could not find an affordable notary nearby.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports the claim that lack of notary access delays paperwork.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Offer Free Notary Hours.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Easier access to a notary can help residents complete necessary paperwork.
choices.B: Every resident needs legal advice before signing any document.
choices.C: Notarized signatures are never required for ordinary tasks.
choices.D: Community centers are too unfamiliar for residents to use.

questions.correctAnswerText: Easier access to a notary can help residents complete necessary paperwork.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that notary access can remove a practical barrier.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Practice Room
Tara had cleaned the dance studio every evening for nearly a year, sliding dust mops across the wooden floor after students went home. She knew the rhythm of every class from the hallway: tap on Mondays, ballroom on Wednesdays, children’s ballet on Saturdays. She also knew which mirror had a crack near the corner and which window rattled when buses passed.
One rainy Thursday, Ms. Inez found Tara lingering near the doorway after the beginner salsa class. “You keep time better than some people who pay me,” she said. “Do you want to try?”
Tara laughed too quickly. “I work here.”
“That was not my question.”
The next week, Tara stayed after her shift. The room felt different without a mop handle in her hands. She stood at the back of the class, trying to copy the steps without looking as nervous as she felt. Left, right, pause. Back, forward, turn. Her shoes squeaked. Twice, she moved the wrong way and nearly bumped the radiator.
At the end, Ms. Inez asked everyone to repeat the first pattern. This time Tara counted softly under her breath. She made one turn without stopping, then another. When the music ended, she looked automatically toward the mirrors, expecting to see proof that she did not belong.
Instead, she saw the cracked corner, the rattling window, and herself still standing in the room.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Tara must overcome the belief that she does not belong as a student in the dance studio.
choices.B: Tara tries to repair the cracked mirror before class begins.
choices.C: Tara wants Ms. Inez to cancel the salsa class.
choices.D: Tara argues with other students about the music.

questions.correctAnswerText: Tara must overcome the belief that she does not belong as a student in the dance studio.
questions.explanation: Tara initially sees herself only as an employee but begins to participate as a student.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Practice Room.”
Why does Ms. Inez say, “That was not my question”?

choices.A: She wants Tara to answer whether she wants to try dancing, not explain her job role.
choices.B: She is asking Tara to clean the studio faster.
choices.C: She does not recognize Tara as an employee.
choices.D: She wants Tara to leave before class starts.

questions.correctAnswerText: She wants Tara to answer whether she wants to try dancing, not explain her job role.
questions.explanation: Ms. Inez challenges Tara’s assumption that working at the studio means she cannot join the class.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Practice Room.”
What does Tara’s counting softly under her breath show?

choices.A: She is using a strategy to help herself learn the steps.
choices.B: She is trying to interrupt the instructor.
choices.C: She has decided to stop dancing immediately.
choices.D: She is reading a cleaning checklist aloud.

questions.correctAnswerText: She is using a strategy to help herself learn the steps.
questions.explanation: Counting softly helps Tara follow the rhythm and complete the movement.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Practice Room.”
Which TWO details show Tara’s nervousness?

choices.A: She laughs too quickly when Ms. Inez asks if she wants to try.
choices.B: She tries to copy the steps without looking as nervous as she feels.
choices.C: She knows which mirror has a crack near the corner.
choices.D: She has cleaned the studio for nearly a year.
choices.E: She hears buses pass the window.

questions.correctAnswerText: She laughs too quickly when Ms. Inez asks if she wants to try. | She tries to copy the steps without looking as nervous as she feels.
questions.explanation: Both details show Tara’s nervousness about joining the class.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Practice Room.”
What does the final sentence suggest?

choices.A: Tara begins to see herself as someone who can belong in the class.
choices.B: Tara decides that the studio should be closed permanently.
choices.C: Tara realizes that the mirrors have all been replaced.
choices.D: Tara plans to stop cleaning the studio immediately.

questions.correctAnswerText: Tara begins to see herself as someone who can belong in the class.
questions.explanation: The final image shows Tara still standing in the room after participating, suggesting a shift in self-perception.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Practice Room.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.B: Tara counts softly under her breath.
tiles.C: Tara makes one turn without stopping.
tiles.D: Tara locks the studio before the lesson starts.

targets.T1: Evidence that Tara sees herself mainly as an employee at first
targets.T2: Evidence that Tara uses a learning strategy

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Tara says, “I work here.”; T2=Tara counts softly under her breath.; T3=Tara makes one turn without stopping.
questions.explanation: The tiles show Tara’s initial self-view, her strategy, and her progress.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Reading a Lease Renewal Notice
A lease renewal notice tells a tenant what choices are available when a rental agreement is close to ending. The notice may offer a new fixed-term lease, explain a rent change, describe a month-to-month option, or state the deadline for responding. Reading the notice carefully can help tenants avoid confusion and unexpected costs.
One important detail is the response date. If a tenant misses the deadline, the lease may automatically change to month-to-month status, renew under stated terms, or end, depending on the original agreement and local rules. Tenants should not assume the deadline is flexible. If they need more time, they should contact the landlord or property manager in writing.
Another detail is the difference between rent and fees. A notice may list the new monthly rent but mention separate charges for parking, pets, storage, or late payment. A tenant comparing options should calculate the full monthly cost, not just the rent line. If any charge is unclear, the tenant should ask for written clarification before signing.
A lease renewal notice is not the same as legal advice. Tenants with serious concerns may need help from a housing counselor or attorney. Still, careful reading can help tenants understand dates, costs, and choices before making a decision that affects where they live.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A lease renewal notice gives important information about deadlines, costs, and rental choices.
choices.B: A lease renewal notice always means a tenant must move immediately.
choices.C: Tenants should ignore fees when comparing rental options.
choices.D: Landlords are never allowed to change rent at renewal time.

questions.correctAnswerText: A lease renewal notice gives important information about deadlines, costs, and rental choices.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how renewal notices help tenants understand options, dates, and costs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Lease Renewal Notice.”
Why is the response date important?

choices.A: Missing it may affect whether the lease renews, changes, or ends.
choices.B: It tells tenants when the building was constructed.
choices.C: It shows which neighbor signed a lease first.
choices.D: It replaces the need to read the rest of the notice.

questions.correctAnswerText: Missing it may affect whether the lease renews, changes, or ends.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that missing the response date can affect the lease outcome.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Lease Renewal Notice.”
What should tenants consider when comparing rental options?

choices.A: The full monthly cost, including rent and separate fees
choices.B: Only the color of the notice paper
choices.C: The landlord’s favorite type of parking space
choices.D: Only the previous rent amount, regardless of new charges

questions.correctAnswerText: The full monthly cost, including rent and separate fees
questions.explanation: The passage advises tenants to consider rent plus fees when comparing options.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Lease Renewal Notice.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that tenants should get unclear information in writing?

choices.A: “A lease renewal notice tells a tenant what choices are available when a rental agreement is close to ending.”
choices.B: “If any charge is unclear, the tenant should ask for written clarification before signing.”
choices.C: “A notice may list the new monthly rent but mention separate charges.”
choices.D: “A lease renewal notice is not the same as legal advice.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “If any charge is unclear, the tenant should ask for written clarification before signing.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly supports the idea of getting unclear information in writing.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Lease Renewal Notice.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains what a renewal notice is, identifies key details, and advises careful review.
choices.B: It tells a story about one tenant moving into a new apartment.
choices.C: It compares rental buildings in several different cities.
choices.D: It argues that tenants should never renew a lease.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains what a renewal notice is, identifies key details, and advises careful review.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the notice, discusses response dates and costs, and recommends careful reading.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Community Repair Hotline
(1) The town will open a community repair hotline for residents reporting damaged sidewalks, broken street signs, and blocked storm drains. (2) Callers should provide the nearest address or intersection and describe the problem briefly. (3) Reports with clear locations are easier for crews to investigate. (4) [D1], callers should mention whether the problem creates an immediate safety concern. (5) Each report [D2] assigned a tracking number. (6) Residents may use the number to ask for updates later.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In addition; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “In addition” adds another instruction, and “will be assigned” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Community Repair Hotline.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Residents may use the number to ask for updates later.
choices.B: Residents may use the number for the purpose of asking for updates at a later time.
choices.C: The number may be used by residents to make later requests for updates that are later.
choices.D: Residents, who are residents, may use the number later when updates are asked for.

questions.correctAnswerText: Residents may use the number to ask for updates later.
questions.explanation: This choice is concise and preserves the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The reports from several streets needs review.
choices.B: The list of blocked drains are on the supervisor’s desk.
choices.C: The crews who repair sidewalks arrive before noon.
choices.D: The map of damaged signs have been updated.

questions.correctAnswerText: The crews who repair sidewalks arrive before noon.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “crews” correctly takes the plural verb “arrive.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Luis called Ethan after the inspection, he filed the report.
choices.B: Luis called Ethan after the inspection, and Luis filed the report.
choices.C: He filed it when Luis called Ethan after the inspection.
choices.D: After the inspection, he called him and filed it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Luis called Ethan after the inspection, and Luis filed the report.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Luis” makes clear who filed the report.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The crew expected the repair to take one hour. [D1], hidden damage under the pavement made the work last all afternoon.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=However
questions.explanation: “However” signals contrast between the expected repair time and the delay.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The hotline received many calls; however, crews responded to the most urgent reports first.
choices.B: The hotline received many calls however crews responded to the most urgent reports first.
choices.C: The hotline received many calls, however crews responded to the most urgent reports first.
choices.D: The hotline received many calls; however crews responded, to the most urgent reports first.

questions.correctAnswerText: The hotline received many calls; however, crews responded to the most urgent reports first.
questions.explanation: A semicolon before “however” and a comma after it correctly connect the clauses.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because several signs were damaged during the storm.
choices.B: After the repair crew checked the intersection.
choices.C: The supervisor assigned two workers to the blocked drain.
choices.D: While waiting for the replacement sign.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor assigned two workers to the blocked drain.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The dispatcher logged the call and sends the crew yesterday.
choices.B: The dispatcher logs the call and sent the crew yesterday.
choices.C: The dispatcher logged the call and sent the crew yesterday.
choices.D: The dispatcher had logged the call and sends the crew yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The dispatcher logged the call and sent the crew yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The form asks for the location, a short description, and whether the issue is urgent.
choices.B: The form asks for the location, issue description, and urgency status.
choices.C: The form asks for locating the issue, a description that is short, and urgency.
choices.D: The form asks for the location, describing the issue, and to say it is urgent.

questions.correctAnswerText: The form asks for the location, issue description, and urgency status.
questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Allow Food Trucks on Main Street
The city should create designated food truck spaces on Main Street during weekday lunch hours. Office workers, students, courthouse visitors, and construction crews often have only a short break. Food trucks would provide quick, varied meals without requiring people to drive across town. A few marked spaces would bring more activity to the area and give small food businesses a chance to reach customers.
Food trucks can also support existing businesses. People who come downtown for lunch may stop at a bookstore, pharmacy, or coffee shop afterward. The city could require trucks to use assigned spaces, follow health rules, pay a permit fee, and operate only during certain hours. These limits would prevent trucks from blocking sidewalks or staying all day.
Some restaurant owners worry about competition. That concern is understandable, but competition already exists among restaurants, cafés, and grocery prepared-food counters. A limited food truck program would add choices without replacing permanent restaurants. The city should test the idea for six months and review traffic, sales, and complaints before making it permanent.
Passage B: Protect Main Street Restaurants First
The city should not add food truck spaces on Main Street during lunch hours. Downtown restaurants pay rent, property-related costs, utilities, insurance, and year-round staffing expenses. Food trucks have lower costs and could park near restaurant doors during the busiest part of the day. That is not a fair balance.
Main Street also has limited curb space. Delivery drivers, customers with disabilities, buses, and emergency vehicles already compete for access. Adding food trucks could increase congestion and make it harder for people to reach the very businesses the city wants to support. Sidewalk crowding may also become a problem near courthouse entrances and crosswalks.
The city can support small food businesses in other ways. It could invite food trucks to evening festivals, weekend markets, or underused lots outside the busiest blocks. These options would give vendors access to customers without disrupting lunch service for permanent restaurants. A healthy downtown depends on businesses that invest in the area all year, so the city should protect them before adding new curbside competition.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports a limited weekday food truck program on Main Street, while Passage B argues that the city should protect restaurants and curb access first.
choices.B: Passage A argues that restaurants should close permanently, while Passage B argues that food should not be sold downtown.
choices.C: Passage A supports banning all small businesses, while Passage B supports removing sidewalks.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on school schedules, while Passage B focuses on apartment leases.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports a limited weekday food truck program on Main Street, while Passage B argues that the city should protect restaurants and curb access first.
questions.explanation: Passage A favors a test program; Passage B emphasizes restaurant fairness and congestion concerns.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about food trucks on Main Street.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: Workers, students, courthouse visitors, and construction crews often have only a short lunch break.
choices.B: Downtown restaurants pay rent and year-round staffing costs.
choices.C: Main Street has limited curb space.
choices.D: Delivery drivers and buses compete for access.

questions.correctAnswerText: Workers, students, courthouse visitors, and construction crews often have only a short lunch break.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports Passage A’s claim that quick food options would be useful downtown.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about food trucks on Main Street.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Food trucks could create unfair competition for restaurants with higher fixed costs.
choices.B: Food trucks could increase congestion in already limited curb space.
choices.C: Food trucks are always unsafe regardless of health rules.
choices.D: Main Street has unlimited parking at all hours.
choices.E: Restaurants never benefit from downtown foot traffic.

questions.correctAnswerText: Food trucks could create unfair competition for restaurants with higher fixed costs. | Food trucks could increase congestion in already limited curb space.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims with specific reasons.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about food trucks on Main Street.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses concerns about blocking sidewalks or staying too long?

choices.A: “Office workers, students, courthouse visitors, and construction crews often have only a short break.”
choices.B: “A few marked spaces would bring more activity to the area.”
choices.C: “The city could require trucks to use assigned spaces, follow health rules, pay a permit fee, and operate only during certain hours.”
choices.D: “Competition already exists among restaurants, cafés, and grocery prepared-food counters.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The city could require trucks to use assigned spaces, follow health rules, pay a permit fee, and operate only during certain hours.”
questions.explanation: This sentence addresses concerns about blocking sidewalks, safety, and overuse of curb space.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about food trucks on Main Street.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors care about downtown business activity, but they disagree about whether food trucks would help or harm it.
choices.B: Both authors believe Main Street should stop serving lunch.
choices.C: Both authors argue that courthouse visitors should bring meals from home.
choices.D: Both authors think permit rules are impossible to enforce.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors care about downtown business activity, but they disagree about whether food trucks would help or harm it.
questions.explanation: Passage A sees food trucks as adding activity, while Passage B sees them as potential competition and congestion.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about food trucks on Main Street.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on lunchtime foot traffic, curb-space use, restaurant sales, and customer demand for food trucks
choices.B: A list of recipes used by restaurants outside the city
choices.C: A history of courthouse architecture
choices.D: A survey asking residents their favorite dinner music

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on lunchtime foot traffic, curb-space use, restaurant sales, and customer demand for food trucks
questions.explanation: This information would help evaluate both economic and practical claims.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Marked with orange paint, Carla photographed the cracked sidewalk.

choices.A: Marked with orange paint, Carla photographed the cracked sidewalk.
choices.B: Carla photographed the cracked sidewalk marked with orange paint.
choices.C: Carla, marked with orange paint, photographed the cracked sidewalk.
choices.D: The orange paint photographed Carla and the cracked sidewalk.

questions.correctAnswerText: Carla photographed the cracked sidewalk marked with orange paint.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “sidewalk,” the thing marked with orange paint.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The city opened a hotline. The hotline accepts repair reports.

choices.A: The city opened a hotline that accepts repair reports.
choices.B: The hotline opened the city and accepts repair reports.
choices.C: Accepting the city, repair reports opened a hotline.
choices.D: The city opened, and repair reports accepted the hotline.

questions.correctAnswerText: The city opened a hotline that accepts repair reports.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The manager’s instructions were posted beside the time clock.
choices.B: The managers instructions were posted beside the time clock.
choices.C: The managers’ instruction’s were posted beside the time clock.
choices.D: The manager instructions’ were posted beside the time clock.

questions.correctAnswerText: The manager’s instructions were posted beside the time clock.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the instructions belong to one manager.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Start High School Later
High schools should begin later in the morning. Teenagers often have sleep patterns that make it difficult to fall asleep early, yet many high schools start before 8:00 a.m. Students who arrive tired may struggle to concentrate, remember information, and manage stress. A later start time would better match students’ sleep needs and improve the school day.
The benefits could reach beyond academics. Tired students are more likely to be late, miss school, or feel overwhelmed. A schedule that allows more sleep may improve attendance and mood. Some schools that moved start times later reported fewer morning tardies and more alert students in first-period classes. Students still need good habits, but the schedule should not make healthy sleep harder.
Changing the start time would require planning. Bus routes, after-school activities, sports practices, and family schedules would need adjustment. These challenges are real, but they are not impossible. Districts can shift elementary and high school bus times, coordinate with coaches, and give families several months of notice. Schools already change schedules for construction, weather, and budget reasons. Student health and learning are important enough to justify similar effort.
A later start time is not a cure for every problem, but it is a practical change that supports students’ ability to learn.
Passage B:
Keep the Current Start Time
High schools should keep the current start time. Starting later may sound helpful, but it creates problems that affect families, transportation, and after-school responsibilities. Many students work part-time jobs, care for younger siblings, attend athletic practice, or participate in clubs. If school ends later, these activities may be pushed into the evening, leaving students with less time for homework, dinner, and family responsibilities.
Transportation is another concern. Districts often use the same buses for elementary, middle, and high school routes. Moving high school later may force younger children to wait for buses earlier in the morning or may require expensive new transportation plans. Families that depend on older students to help younger siblings after school could also face new childcare problems.
Supporters of later start times assume students will use the extra morning time for sleep, but that is not guaranteed. Some students may stay up later if they know school starts later. Without education about sleep habits and limits on late-night screen use, a schedule change may produce less benefit than expected.
Schools should teach students about sleep and encourage healthy routines, but changing the entire daily schedule may create more disruption than improvement.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether high schools should begin later in the morning? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
